name: Metformin reduced older-adult muscle senescence/SASP markers after disuse
summary: A small older-adult bed-rest study gives metformin a direct human senescence/SASP biomarker signal in a stress-recovery setting.
statement: In healthy older adults exposed to bed rest and re-ambulation, metformin pre-treatment was associated with less type-I myofiber atrophy during disuse, lower pro-inflammatory transcriptional profiles, lower collagen deposition during recovery, and reduced skeletal-muscle cellular senescence/SASP markers.
population_or_model: Healthy adults aged 60 years and older undergoing short bed rest and recovery
time_horizon: Two-week metformin run-in, five days of bed rest, and seven days of recovery after metformin withdrawal
quantitative_note: The PubMed abstract reports a two-arm controlled trial with n = 20.
caveats: The study was small and used an acute disuse/recovery model rather than a long-term aging endpoint.
Metformin is pleiotropic, so attribution specifically to senomorphic SASP modulation is plausible but not isolated.
The finding should not override other metformin functional-boundary results in older adults.
